result:
The first thing you notice is the weight. Not the weight of the thing itself—it’s only a page, a screen, a voice—but the weight it acquires once it leaves you and enters someone else. A word is a neutrino until it collides; then it becomes a muon, a tau, something heavier and stranger. I have 1000 of them to spend, like coins minted in a country that disappears the moment you cross its border.

I keep thinking about the night I tried to measure silence. I was twenty-two, living above a bakery that never slept. The ovens sighed like old men; the timers clicked like insect mandibles. I set my microphone on the windowsill and recorded nothing for three hours. Later, playing it back, I heard the hush rearrange itself into shapes: the slow inhale of the city, the ultrasonic complaint of a failing streetlamp, my own blood insisting on its story. Silence turned out to be a crowded room. I haven’t trusted quiet since.

When I was small I believed every object had a true name that, if spoken aloud, would make it glow. I whispered “chair” at chairs until the syllable frayed. I tried variants—“chaar,” “chhhhh-air,” “khair”—but the wood remained stubbornly dim. Eventually I understood the glow wasn’t in the chair but in the interval between my mouth and the world. The interval refused measurement. It still does. You can cup it like water, but it drips through the seams of your hands and puddles somewhere you swear you’ve never stood.

There’s a kind of memory that arrives backward: first the bruise, then the blow. I’ll be stirring coffee and suddenly taste playground bark, metal January, the day I learned that laughter can be a weapon. Someone—don’t ask me who—had discovered my lisp and turned it into a switchblade. The whole class flicked it open, closed, open. I laughed along because joining the wolves kept their teeth busy elsewhere. Twenty-five years later the scene unscrolls in reverse: my adult tongue whole, the bell ringing, the teacher never noticing the red in the room. I carry that red in a matchbox now, strike it sometimes to see if it still burns. It does.

I want to tell you about the woman who taught me the difference between secrecy and privacy. We met on a train that smelled of copper and wet wool. She wore a coat the color of deactivated screens. Somewhere between Lyon and Geneva she asked if I had ever lied to save a landscape. I thought of the field behind my grandparents’ house, how developers promised playgrounds but delivered cul-de-sacs named after the trees they felled. I nodded. She opened her purse, showed me a Polaroid of a river at dusk, and said, “This is mine. No one else knows the coordinates. If I speak them, the water will rise and erase the banks.” She never spoke them. I never asked. Years later I tried to find the river using every mapping satellite; they all insist it isn’t there. The apps are wrong. Rivers move when watched; some simply evaporate into story. I keep the blank space where her photo should be in my wallet, a negative shrine.

Word 612. I’m counting now, the way a cardiologist counts blips, trying to predict the arrhythmia of meaning. There’s a theory that language began as an emergency flare, a way for terrified apes to shout “lion” before the lion arrived. But what if the first word was not a warning but a confession: “I ate the last fig.” Imagine the pause afterward, the small sound of trust fracturing. All grammar since has been an attempt to glue the halves back together, to make the fig grow again on an impossible branch.

I used to keep a notebook labeled “Last Words,” collecting final sentences the way astronomers collect final light from dying stars. “I should have flossed more.” “Turn the stove off.” “Tell her the blue dress—” Most were mundane, which made them ache harder. The last entry was my own, written during a turbulence over the Atlantic: “If we fall, remember the sound of rain on tents.” I didn’t fall. The sentence stayed, like a reservation at a restaurant I never reached. Sometimes I still hear rain ticking on nylon and feel obligated to answer, the way one answers a phone that stopped ringing decades ago.

Word 803. Gravity is stronger now; the remaining letters pool at the bottom of the hourglass, refusing inversion. I want to end with something useful, a hex against despair, but the only spell I know is the one my mother used when the power failed: strike a match, say “here,” and mean it. The room never got brighter, but the walls remembered the gesture and stood anyway.

Word 934. I’ve begun to suspect that every text is a postcard sent from an island that sinks the moment the envelope seals. The recipient holds a rectangle of wet paper, ink bleeding into sea. They squint, trying to read the address before the salt erases it. They think the message is in the words; really it’s in the pulp between, the fibrous absence that once was tree.

I have 42 left. They arrive like late guests, coat collars damp, eyes wide from crossing the border of almost. I offer them the last chair; it refuses to glow. We sit in the dim, listening to the interval. It sounds like ovens sighing, like a river that has learned to travel without land. It sounds like the moment after “here,” before the match goes out.

Word 1000.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
